title: Minimal superfluid vortices in chiral perturbation theory
authors: Fabrizio Canfora, Martın Garrido, Massimo Mannarelli, Anibal Neira
abstract: We derive some properties of rotational vortices in the pion condensed phase. Employing leading order chiral perturbation theory we determine the minimal energy condition for vortex nucleation. Vortices have quantized angular momentum along the rotation axis, an hallmark of superfluidity, and self-confine pions. The critical rotation frequency for vortex nucleation is estimated.
summary: This theoretical paper studies superfluid vortices in a pion condensate using Chiral Perturbation Theory. By applying the BPS bound, the authors derive the energy cost and quantized angular momentum associated with these vortices. The results confirm superfluidity and estimate the critical rotation frequency required for vortex formation.
main_problem: The paper investigates the properties of superfluid vortices that can form in a pion condensed phase, which is analogous to a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC). It aims to determine the minimal energy required for these vortices to nucleate.
main_result: The theory confirms that the pion condensate supports quantized vortices, and the energy cost scales linearly with the winding number, suggesting superfluid behavior. A critical rotation frequency for nucleation is estimated.
method: The analysis employs leading order Chiral Perturbation Theory (ChPT) and utilizes the Bogomol'nyi-Prasad-Sommerfield (BPS) bound to simplify the free-energy minimization problem for vortex solutions.
model_or_system: The system is a pion gas in the pion condensed phase, modeled using an effective Lagrangian derived from ChPT. The analysis focuses on the field configurations ($\alpha, \Phi, \Theta$) that describe the superfluid order parameter.
key_observables: Quantized angular momentum ($L_z$), free energy ($F$), critical rotation frequency ($\Omega_c$), and the isospin number density ($n_I$).
important_parameters: Pion mass ($m_\pi$), isospin chemical potential ($\mu_I$), and the winding number ($n$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes vanishing temperature, uses the leading order $\chi$PT action, and often simplifies the geometry by assuming cylindrical symmetry and $\varphi_3=0$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the radial dependence of energy density, pressure, and number density for a central vortex, and show the estimated critical frequency dependence on radius.
paper_structure: The paper follows a logical progression: establishing the $\chi$PT setup, deriving the BPS bound, constructing the vortex solutions by solving simplified differential equations, and finally calculating the total free energy and the critical nucleation frequency.
why_interesting: The mathematical framework used (BPS bounds, vortex solutions) is highly relevant to understanding topological defects and superfluid dynamics in ultracold atomic systems, providing a theoretical analogue to BEC vortex physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Modulation of the α function determined by a single vortex at the center, see Eq. (52), as a function of the distance from the z-axis of the cylinder (in units of 1/mπ). The re- ported results are obtained with winding number n = 1, solid black line, n = 5, dashed red line and n = 20, dotted blue line. All the lines intersect at r = 1, α = π/4.

FIG. 2. Values of γ = m2 π/µ2 I that satisfy the global BPS condition, see Eq. (58), as a function of the distance from the z-axis of the cylinder (in units of 1/mπ). The reported results are obtained with winding number n = 1, solid black line, n = 2, dashed red line, n = 3, dotted blue line, and n = 4, dot-dashed green line.
FIG. 4. Radial dependence of the isospin number density for vortices with winding number n = 1, R = 10 (solid black) and R = 20 (dashed red) normalized to 16KµI. Distances are in units of 1/mπ.

FIG. 3. Pressure and energy density determined by a central vortex with winding number n = 1 (in units of 2Km2 π), as a function of the distance from the z-axis of the cylinder (in units of 1/mπ). The results are obtained using the global BPS condition, see Eqs. (58) and (61) with R = 10,. Top panel the pressure in the xy-plane is in black, while the pressure along the z–direction is the dashed red line. Bottom panel, energy density.

FIG. 5. Radial dependence of the energy density, pressure (in units of 16Km2 π) and number density (in units of 16Kmπ). Results obtained with a central vortex with winding number n = 1 using the local BPS condition, see the main text for more details. Distances are in units of 1/mπ.
